G001_wa_topic.title: は — topic marker
G001_wa_topic.short: Marks the topic of the sentence. Pronounced 'wa', not 'ha'.
G001_wa_topic.long: The topic particle は signals what the sentence is ABOUT. It sets the stage for what follows. Often translatable as 'as for X' or 'speaking of X'. It is distinct from the subject marker が — は can replace が but adds a sense of contrast or emphasis on the topic.
examples: 私は学生です — As for me, [I] am a student.
雨は静かです — The rain is quiet.
今朝は雨です — This morning, it is raining.
G001_wa_topic.notes: Most fundamental particle. Always pronounced 'wa' here, never 'ha'. Authoring tip: pair every new noun with は in its first appearance to set the topic clearly.

G002_ga_subject.title: が — subject marker
G002_ga_subject.short: Subject marker. Contrasts with は: が emphasizes the subject; は sets the topic.
G002_ga_subject.long: The subject particle が marks who or what performs the action or holds the state. Unlike は, が focuses attention on the subject itself — used when introducing new information ('a cat is there'), answering 'who/what' questions, or with stative verbs of existence (います/あります). Rule of thumb: は = old/topic information; が = new/identifying information.
examples: 猫がいます — There IS a cat. (newly introducing the cat)
雨が来ます — The rain comes.
風が静かです — The wind is quiet.
G002_ga_subject.notes: Authoring tip: introduce が only after は is established. The pedagogical point of が in beginner stories is the は↔が contrast — use が to introduce a NEW entity in the scene, then switch to は to keep talking about it.

G003_desu.title: です — polite copula
G003_desu.short: Polite form of 'to be'. Links a topic to a description.
G003_desu.long: です is the polite copula. It comes at the end of a sentence to link a noun or adjective to the topic. 'X は Y です' means 'X is Y' (politely). For i-adjectives, です follows the adjective directly. For na-adjectives, です follows the stem.
examples: 雨です — It is raining.
私は学生です — I am a student.
猫は静かです — The cat is quiet.
G003_desu.notes: Most-used grammar in our library (40+ uses). Authoring tip: です attaches to nouns and na-adjectives directly; for i-adjectives, です follows the adjective (いいです). For past tense, use でした (G013), not い-adj past form.

G004_ni_location.title: に — location / direction marker
G004_ni_location.short: Marks the location of existence or the direction of movement.
G004_ni_location.long: に has several uses. As a location marker, it indicates where something exists (います/あります). As a direction marker, it indicates where something or someone is going. It is more precise than で, which marks the location of an action.
examples: 猫は椅子にいます — The cat is on the chair. (location of existence)
公園に行きます — I go to the park. (direction of motion)
机に本があります — There is a book on the desk.
G004_ni_location.notes: Re-anchored to story_9 where に-particle appears 4 times — gives the intro story the same-story redundancy needed for pattern recognition (formerly story_6 which only had 1 に).

G005_wo_object.title: を — direct object marker
G005_wo_object.short: Marks the direct object of a verb.
G005_wo_object.long: The particle を (pronounced 'o') marks the direct object — the thing that receives the action of the verb. 'X を verb' means 'to verb X'. It is one of the most fundamental particles in Japanese.
examples: 本を読みます — I read a book.
お茶を飲みます — I drink tea.
月を見ます — I look at the moon.
G005_wo_object.notes: Authoring tip: pronounced 'o', not 'wo'. Always between the direct object and a transitive verb. Don't use with intransitive verbs (います, あります — those take が or に).

G006_kara_from.title: から — from
G006_kara_from.short: Marks a starting point in space, time, or reason.
G006_kara_from.long: から indicates the origin or starting point of something. In space: 'まどから' means 'from the window'. In time: 'あさから' means 'from the morning'. It can also mean 'because' when following a clause.
examples: 友達から手紙が来ます — A letter comes from my friend.
朝から雨です — It has been raining since morning.
窓から月を見ます — I look at the moon through the window.
G006_kara_from.notes: Authoring tip: から marks origin (place, time, or person). Distinct from まで (until). Different から meaning 'because' (clause + から) is a separate point and not yet in scope.

G007_te_form.title: て-form — connective verb form
G007_te_form.short: Connects clauses or acts as a base for compound forms like て-います.
G007_te_form.long: The て-form (te-form) is a multipurpose inflection. It can connect two clauses ('I did X and then Y'), make requests (〜てください), or form ongoing-state expressions with います. Formation rules vary by verb class: ichidan verbs drop る and add て; godan verbs undergo a consonant shift.
examples: 雨が来て、風が来ます — The rain comes, and then the wind comes.
本を読んで、お茶を飲みます — I read a book and drink tea.
見て、思います — I see [it] and think.
G007_te_form.notes: Authoring tip: te-form has many uses. We currently use it as a sentence connector ('A and then B'). It's also the base for ています (G008), てください (request), and many later forms. Conjugation depends on verb class: ichidan → drop る, add て (見る → 見て). Godan → varies by stem.

G008_te_iru.title: 〜ています — ongoing state / action in progress
G008_te_iru.short: Expresses an action in progress or a resulting state.
G008_te_iru.long: て-form + います describes either an ongoing action ('is doing') or the state that results from a completed action ('is [already] done'). ぬれています means 'is (in a state of being) wet'. きています could mean 'has come and is here'. Context determines which reading applies.
examples: 猫は寝ています — The cat is sleeping. (action in progress)
雨が降っています — It is raining.
ドアが開いています — The door is open. (resulting state)
G008_te_iru.notes: Authoring tip: ています has TWO meanings depending on the verb: (1) action in progress for activity verbs (read, walk, eat); (2) resulting state for change verbs (open, die, marry). Get this wrong and the meaning flips. Underused in our library (only 2 uses); good candidate for future stories.

G009_mo_also.title: も — also / too
G009_mo_also.short: Marks something as additional. Replaces は or が when 'X too / X also' is meant.
G009_mo_also.long: The particle も attaches to a noun and means 'also' or 'too'. It typically replaces the topic particle は or the subject particle が — you do not stack them. 'AもBも〜' means 'both A and B 〜'. With negatives, it can mean 'not even X'. Pronounced 'mo'.
examples: 私も雨を見ます — I, too, look at the rain.
猫もお茶も静かです — Both the cat and the tea are quiet.
友達も本を読みます — My friend also reads a book.
G009_mo_also.notes: Authoring tip: も REPLACES は or が — never stack them (NOT 私はも). 'AもBも〜' = 'both A and B'. With negative verbs, AもBも〜ない = 'neither A nor B'. Heavy in our library (14 uses).

G010_to_and.title: と — and (exhaustive list)
G010_to_and.short: Connects two or more nouns into a complete list ('A and B').
G010_to_and.long: The particle と links nouns to form an exhaustive 'and' list: 'AとB' means 'A and B (and only those)'. It is used between every pair, e.g. 'AとBとC'. Distinct from や, which gives a non-exhaustive 'A, B, etc.' Pronounced 'to'. Comes between nouns; it is not used between verbs or full clauses.
examples: 猫と私 — the cat and I
本とお茶 — books and tea
風と雨 — wind and rain
G010_to_and.notes: Authoring tip: と is exhaustive ('A and B and only those'). Distinct from や (G011, non-exhaustive 'A, B, and so on'). Always between every pair: AとBとC. Don't use と to connect verbs or sentences (use て-form for that). と also functions as a quote particle (G014).

G011_ya_partial.title: や — and (partial / non-exhaustive list)
G011_ya_partial.short: Lists nouns as a non-exhaustive 'A, B, and so on'.
G011_ya_partial.long: The particle や connects nouns in a non-exhaustive list, implying 'and others'. 'AやB' means 'A, B, and similar things'. Distinct from と (exhaustive 'and only'). Often closed with 'など' for emphasis. Comes between nouns; not used between verbs or full clauses.
examples: 月や星があります — There are the moon, the stars, and so on.
本やお茶 — books, tea, and similar things
猫や友達がいます — there are cats, friends, and others
G011_ya_partial.notes: Authoring tip: や implies the list is INCOMPLETE — there are other items too. Often closed with など for emphasis (本やお茶など). Underused in our library (only 4 uses); good for atmospheric descriptions where 'and other things' fits naturally.

G012_soshite_then.title: そして — and then
G012_soshite_then.short: Sentence-initial connector meaning 'and then', linking sequential clauses.
G012_soshite_then.long: そして is an adverb-style connector placed at the start of a new sentence to mean 'and then' or 'after that'. It chains events or observations together temporally and is more formal/literary than a comma alone. Unlike 'and' (と) which joins nouns, そして joins entire sentences. Always followed by a comma in modern writing.
examples: 雨です。そして、風です — It is raining. And then, the wind.
本を読みます。そして、お茶を飲みます — I read a book. And then, I drink tea.
猫は静かです。そして、月も静かです — The cat is quiet. And then, the moon is also quiet.
G012_soshite_then.notes: Authoring tip: sentence-initial connector for sequential events or observations. Always followed by a comma in our convention. Don't overuse — every story currently uses そして 1-2× as the structural pivot. Often paired with でした (past) for narrative chaining.

G013_mashita_past.title: 〜ました — polite past tense
G013_mashita_past.short: Past tense of polite verbs and です: 〜ます becomes 〜ました, です becomes でした.
G013_mashita_past.long: Polite past in Japanese is formed by replacing the 〜ます ending of a polite verb with 〜ました, and by replacing です with でした for nouns and na-adjectives. This is the first verb form a learner encounters that requires real conjugation thought. Examples: 見ます → 見ました ('saw'), 来ます → 来ました ('came'), 雨です → 雨でした ('it was raining'). The negative past is 〜ませんでした (not yet introduced). Plain (dictionary) past 〜た is a separate point (N5_ta_form, also untaught).
examples: 雨でした — It was raining.
私は手紙を読みました — I read the letter.
友達から手紙が来ました — A letter came from my friend.
G013_mashita_past.notes: Authoring tip: ました is the 'narrative past' tense — perfect for retrospective stories, letters, recollections. Don't mix tenses freely within a single story; commit to a tense per scene. Story 11 was our first all-past-tense piece.

G014_to_omoimasu.title: 〜と思います
G014_to_omoimasu.short: I think that ~ / I think it is ~
G014_to_omoimasu.long: Quoted-thought construction. The と particle marks what is thought (or said), and 思います is the polite form of 思う ('to think'). Attaches to plain forms (e.g. 雨だ, 静かだ, いい朝だ); the copula だ is required before と when the thought ends in a noun or na-adjective. For verbs, attach と直接 to the plain form (e.g. 来ると思います = 'I think it will come'). Distinct from と言います ('says that ~') which uses 言う instead of 思う. First N4 grammar point in our library.
examples: 雨だと思います — I think it is raining.
猫はいい友達だと思います — I think the cat is a good friend.
友達も来ると思います — I think my friend will also come.
G014_to_omoimasu.notes: Authoring tip: と思います lets the narrator speculate or interpret — opens up a new dimension of voice. The と here is the QUOTE particle (different function from G010 'and-list'). Authoring tip: write your thought clause first as a stand-alone sentence (ends in だ/です), then add と思います.

G015_no_possessive.title: の — possessive / attributive
G015_no_possessive.short: Links nouns: A の B = 'B of A' or 'A's B'.
G015_no_possessive.long: の links two nouns. The basic meaning is possession or attribution: 私の本 = 'my book' (literally 'I-of-book'). It also expresses kind/category: 友達の朝 = 'a friend's morning' or 'morning of a friend'. The first noun modifies the second. Distinct from の-nominalizer (N4) which turns clauses into nouns.
examples: 私の本 — my book
友達の朝 — a friend's morning
雨の窓 — a rainy window (literally 'window of rain')
G015_no_possessive.notes: Authoring tip: の chains are pure noun-phrase building. Don't introduce until the learner has at least two nouns to combine — i.e. story 1 onwards. We've been silently mis-tagging の as G001_wa_topic since story 1; this entry retroactively classifies all those uses correctly.

G016_na_adjective.title: な-adjectives — noun-like adjectives
G016_na_adjective.short: Adjectives that take な before a noun (静かな猫). Behave like nouns with the copula.
G016_na_adjective.long: Japanese has two adjective classes: い-adjectives (end in い: いい, 暖かい) and な-adjectives (formally nouns: 静か, 元気). な-adjectives pair with です directly in predicate position (静かです = 'is quiet'), and they require な to attach to a following noun attributively (静かな猫 = 'a quiet cat'). The な does NOT appear in predicate position (NEVER 静かな です), only when modifying a noun. Common na-adjectives in our library: 静か (quiet). Common i-adjectives: いい (good), 暖かい (warm).
examples: 静かな猫 — a quiet cat
静かな雨 — a quiet rain
いい朝です — a good morning is. (compare: い-adj, no な)
G016_na_adjective.notes: Authoring tip: easy to confuse with the topic copula in predicate position. Tag な (between na-adj and noun) as G016, not G003. Stories 5/6/7/9/12 silently mis-tagged this — fixed retroactively.

G017_de_means.title: で — by means / at (location of action)
G017_de_means.short: Marks the means/instrument or the location where an action takes place.
G017_de_means.long: で has two main uses: (1) means/instrument — 'by, with, using' — 鉛筆で書きます ('write with a pencil'); (2) location of action — 'at, in' — 公園で散歩します ('walk in the park'). Distinct from に, which marks location of EXISTENCE (います/あります) or destination of motion. Rule of thumb: で = where something happens; に = where something is.
examples: 公園で散歩します — I walk in the park.
猫は椅子で寝ます — the cat sleeps on the chair.
鉛筆で書きます — I write with a pencil.
G017_de_means.notes: Re-anchored to story_16 (was story_8) so the immediate reinforcement window contains real follow-up uses (で-particle in stories 17, 18, 20, 21).

G021_aru_iru.title: ある / いる — existence verbs
G021_aru_iru.short: ある is for inanimate things ('there is X'); いる is for animate things ('there's a person/animal').
G021_aru_iru.long: Japanese expresses existence with two verbs: ある (inanimate, plants, abstract) and いる (people, animals). Both use the location particle に for where. Polite forms: あります / います.
examples: 机に本があります — There is a book on the desk.
庭に猫がいます — There is a cat in the garden.
友達がいます — I have a friend (lit. a friend exists).
G021_aru_iru.notes: First explicitly introduced in story_7 although あります appears earlier — this is the dedicated focus story for the existence-verb pattern. Listed only G002_ga_subject as a prerequisite (the X が います pattern); に-location (G004) is co-occurring surface in story_7 but is not formally introduced until story_9, so is not a hard prerequisite — the existence-verb pattern is teachable from が alone (友達がいます = 'there is a friend / I have a friend').

G022_i_adj.title: い-adjectives
G022_i_adj.short: Adjectives ending in い conjugate directly (no copula needed in plain form).
G022_i_adj.long: い-adjectives end in い in their dictionary form (大きい, 小さい, 静かい-no-wait-静か-is-na). They attach to nouns directly (大きい家 = a big house) and predicate with です in polite speech (家は大きいです = the house is big). Note: です follows the adjective, not replaces い.
examples: 大きい家です — It is a big house.
小さい鍵 — a small key.
庭は美しいです — The garden is beautiful.
G022_i_adj.notes: Re-anchored to story_18 where 小さい/嬉しい predicate uses anchor a reinforcement window with predicative i-adj in 19-22.

G025_counters.title: Counters
G025_counters.short: Numbers in Japanese take counter suffixes that depend on what is counted.
G025_counters.long: Japanese cannot count nouns directly with bare numbers; a counter suffix is required: 一人 (hitori, one person), 二人 (futari), 三人 (sannin), 一つ (hitotsu, one general thing), 二つ (futatsu), 一回 (ikkai, one time). The choice of counter depends on the category of the noun being counted (people, flat objects, long objects, occurrences, etc.). Beginners learn 〜人 (people), 〜つ (general), and 〜回 (occurrences) first.
examples: 私は一人です — I am alone (one person).
二人で、お茶を飲みます — Two of us drink tea together.
三人の友達 — three friends.
G025_counters.notes: Anchored at story_25 (一人) with reinforcement in stories 28, 29, 30 (二人, 二人, 二人 ×3).

G026_masu_nonpast.title: 〜ます — polite non-past
G026_masu_nonpast.short: Polite non-past verb ending. The default formality for stories and conversation.
G026_masu_nonpast.long: 〜ます is the polite non-past verb ending. It expresses both present habitual ('I read books') and future ('I will read books') depending on context. Stem comes from the verb root: 食べる→食べます, 読む→読みます, する→します, 来る→きます. This story-library has used ます since story 1; story_28 anchors the explicit pattern as the focus of a review story.
examples: 本を買います — I will buy a book.
椅子に座ります — I sit on the chair.
二人で、一緒にお茶を飲みます — Two of us drink tea together.
G026_masu_nonpast.notes: Late-anchored: ます has been used since story 1; story_28 dedicates focus to it.

G018_toki_when.title: 〜とき — 'when' (temporal subordinator)
G018_toki_when.short: Marks a clause that sets the time when the main action happens.
G018_toki_when.long: とき (literally 'time') is a temporal subordinator: 本を読むとき = 'when (I) read a book'. The clause before とき may be plain dictionary form (non-past) or plain past depending on aspect.
examples: 本を読むとき、お茶を飲みます — When (I) read, (I) drink tea.
G018_toki_when.notes: Surface appears only in story_13; not introduced via new_grammar (would fail reinforcement). Kept in state so the library token remains parseable; promote to a real introduction once reinforced.

G019_te_oku.title: 〜ておく — do (and leave done) in advance
G019_te_oku.short: Aspectual auxiliary: do something ahead of time and leave the result.
G019_te_oku.long: Verb-て + おく expresses doing an action in advance and leaving its result in place: 食べておきます = 'I'll eat it (in advance)'. Polite form おきます; plain おく; past おいた.
examples: お茶を入れておきます — I'll prepare the tea in advance.
G019_te_oku.notes: Surface appears only in story_14; not introduced via new_grammar (would fail reinforcement). Kept in state so the library token remains parseable; promote to a real introduction once reinforced.

G027_ni_tsuite.title: 〜について — about / concerning
G027_ni_tsuite.long: Attaches to a noun (or noun phrase) to mark the topic of speech, writing, or thought. In this story it appears in 家族について話します (talks about [her] family) and 庭について書きます (writes about the garden). N3 topical structure; takes a noun directly with no intervening particle.

G028_to_iimasu.title: 〜と言います — says that / is called
G028_to_iimasu.short: Quotes speech or names: X と言います = 'says X' or 'is called X'.
G028_to_iimasu.long: と言います follows a quoted word, phrase, or clause and means 'says (that) ~' or 'is called ~'. The particle と marks the content of the speech or name. In direct quote: 「いい朝です」と言います = 'says "it is a good morning"'. For names: 父は田中と言います = 'Father is called Tanaka'. At this level, use it for simple direct-speech quotes (short phrases the reader already knows), not embedded clauses. Contrast with と思います (think that): 思います is internal, 言います is external/spoken.
examples: 「いい朝です」と言います — Says "it is a good morning".
父は私の名前を言います — Father says my name.
「元気ですか」と言います — Says "are you well?".
G028_to_iimasu.notes: The と here is the same quote particle as in と思います, but paired with 言う (to say) instead of 思う (to think). Use for direct speech and for names (X と言います = is called X).

G029_kute.title: 〜くて — i-adjective te-form (linking)
G029_kute.short: Links i-adjectives: drop い, add くて. 古くて静か = 'old and quiet'.
G029_kute.long: The te-form of i-adjectives is formed by dropping the final い and adding くて. It links two adjectival descriptions of the same subject: 古くて静かな部屋 = 'an old and quiet room', 寒くて長い夜 = 'a cold, long night'. Functions like a coordinating 'and'. Note: いい becomes よくて (irregular). For na-adjectives, use 〜で instead (静かで温かい). Distinct from と for nouns and そして for sentences.
G029_kute.notes: Use 〜くて to chain i-adjectives that describe the same thing. Replaces the worksheet-y X です。Y です。 pattern. For na-adjectives, use 〜で instead.

G030_kara_reason.title: 〜から — because (clause)
G030_kara_reason.short: Reason marker after a clause: 元気だから嬉しい = 'I'm happy because she's well'.
G030_kara_reason.long: から after a clause (verb, adjective, or copula in plain or polite form) gives the reason for what follows. The reason-clause comes first; the result-clause comes after. Distinct from から (from), which attaches to a noun for a starting point. Polite-form usage (元気ですから〜) is acceptable; plain-form (元気だから〜) is more conversational. At this level, use polite-form ですから / verbs in 〜ます〜から to keep register consistent with the surrounding prose.
G030_kara_reason.notes: Disambiguate from から (from): kara-from attaches to a noun (家から = from home); kara-because attaches to a clause (元気ですから = because she's well). Use the surrounding part of speech to tell them apart. Authoring tip: keep the reason in the polite form to match the narration register.

G031_tai.title: 〜たい — want to (verb desire)
G031_tai.short: Verb-stem + たい = 'want to do'. 見たいです = 'I want to see'.
G031_tai.long: Drop the ます from a polite verb (見ます → 見) and add たい to express the speaker's desire to perform that action. The result behaves like an i-adjective (見たい・見たくない・見たかった). Use です for politeness: 見たいです = 'I want to see'. Critically, 〜たい can only describe the speaker's own desire (or, in a question, the listener's). For a third party's desire use 〜たがる. At this level, restrict 〜たい to 私 (the narrator) describing what they want to do. The reason for the desire is often paired with から: 静かですから読みたいです = 'because it's quiet, I want to read'.
G031_tai.notes: First-person desire only. Don't use for father/mother/friend wanting something. The romance arc starts here because 〜たい is the cleanest grammatical surface for *longing*: I want to see, I want to return, I want to know.

G032_demo.title: でも — but (sentence-initial)
G032_demo.short: Sentence-initial contrastive: でも = But (the previous statement is qualified).
G032_demo.long: でも at the start of a sentence introduces a contrast or qualification of the previous sentence. It is the conversational equivalent of English sentence-initial "But...". Use sparingly — too many でも sentences in a row drains the contrast of weight. The grammar is structurally trivial (a sentence-initial connector) but emotionally heavy: でも is how Japanese narration admits hesitation, doubt, or the gap between what one wants and what one does. Distinct from 〜が-but (a clause-level conjunction inside one sentence).
G032_demo.notes: Sentence-initial only at this stage — clause-internal が-but is a separate later grammar. The romance arc needs でも because longing is fundamentally contrastive: I want X, but Y.

G034_ne_confirm.title: ね — sentence-final "right?" / "isn't it?"
G034_ne_confirm.short: Sentence-final ね seeks agreement or shared feeling: いい本ですね = "It's a good book, isn't it."
G034_ne_confirm.long: The sentence-final particle ね expects the listener to share or confirm the speaker's judgment. It is essentially "right?" or "isn't it?" appended to a statement. Crucially, ね is a marker of *shared* perception: the speaker assumes the listener already sees what the speaker sees. This is why it shows up in the library at the moments when characters reach for shared ground — father observing the cold breakfast, the bookstore stranger acknowledging the book, the friend recognizing the kind person. ね is structurally trivial (one syllable, sentence-final) but emotionally precise: it is the grammar of "we are seeing the same thing."
examples: 寒い朝ごはんですね — It's a cold breakfast, isn't it.
いい本ですね — It's a good book, isn't it.
優しい人ですね — A kind person, isn't it.
G034_ne_confirm.notes: Always sentence-final, immediately before the period. Distinguish from よ (which asserts new information the listener doesn't share). Don't over-use: more than one ね per story drains the shared-feeling weight.

G024_da.title: だ — plain copula (before quoting と)
G024_da.short: だ is the plain/casual form of です. Required before と思います / と言います clauses: 元気だと思います = "I think she is well."
G024_da.long: だ is the dictionary/plain form of the copula です. While the library narrates almost entirely in polite (です) form, the quoting particle と grammatically requires the plain copula in front of it: you must say 元気だと思います ("I think she is well"), never 元気ですと思います. The same holds for と言います when quoting a copula clause. This makes だ a structurally necessary form even in a polite-register library — it surfaces precisely at the boundary where one clause becomes the *content* of another. At this level use だ ONLY in this position (before と思います / と言います quoting an attributive clause). Sentence-final だ for plain-form statements is deferred (that is the casual register the library does not yet use).
examples: 猫はいい友達だと思います — I think the cat is a good friend.
友達は元気だと思います — I think the friend is well.
風の朝だと思います — I think it is a windy morning.
G024_da.notes: In this library, だ is used ONLY in the position before と (quoting particle). Sentence-final plain-form だ statements are deferred. Story 29 brings the grammar into focus; earlier appearances in stories 12, 13, 19 are pre-introduction usages forced by the と思います grammar that were tagged as G003_desu before the audit.

G035_arimasen.title: ありません — negative existence (inanimate)
G035_arimasen.short: Negative form of あります for inanimate things: 'there is not / does not exist'.
G035_arimasen.long: ありません is the polite negative of あります and expresses the non-existence of inanimate things. The thing being denied takes は (topic), not が. Example: 鍵はありません (there is no key — about the key). For animate beings (people, animals) the equivalent is いません, the negative of います. This is the library's first introduction of any negation form.

G036_masen.title: 〜ません — polite present negative (verbs)
G036_masen.short: Polite negative form of verbs: 〜ます becomes 〜ません.
G036_masen.long: Replace the polite verb ending 〜ます with 〜ません to negate. 待ちます→待ちません ('do not wait'). 来ます→来ません ('do not come'). The negation applies to the polite present and habitual senses; for past negative use 〜ませんでした (not yet introduced). This is the verb-side complement to G035 ありません — together they let the library deny both events and existences.

G037_ka_question.title: か — sentence-final question marker
G037_ka_question.short: Adding か at the end of a sentence makes it a yes/no or wh-question.
G037_ka_question.long: Adding か to the end of any polite sentence turns it into a question. With a question word like 何 or どこ it is a wh-question (これは何ですか — what is this?); without one it is a yes/no question (元気ですか — are you well?). Spoken Japanese often drops か with rising intonation, but the written register keeps it. The question mark ? is unnecessary and considered Western — か is the punctuation. The library has shipped one untagged か (story 32 s7) which this introduction retroactively governs.

G038_kunai.title: 〜くない — i-adjective negative
G038_kunai.short: Replace the final い of an i-adjective with くない to negate.
G038_kunai.long: い-adjectives negate by replacing the final い with くない: 寒い→寒くない (not cold), 大きい→大きくない (not big). Add です to keep polite register: 寒くないです. The polite alternative 寒くありません is also valid (ありません = polite negative of あります), but 〜くないです is more conversational and is what we introduce here. Note that いい is irregular: いい→よくない. The library has carefully avoided introducing this form until now because it requires both i-adj familiarity (G022) and a comfortable handling of negation (G035, G036) — both now in place.

G039_dare_who.title: 誰 — interrogative 'who'
G039_dare_who.long: 誰 (dare) is the interrogative noun for 'who'. Pairs with です to ask 'who is X?' (Xは誰ですか) or with possessive の (誰の傘 — 'whose umbrella').

G040_doko_where.title: どこ — where
G040_doko_where.long: どこ is the interrogative noun for location. Pairs with です to ask 'where is X?' (Xはどこですか) or with particles to ask directions/positions. Like 何 (W00135), it's both a vocabulary word and a grammar marker — story 48 introduces it lexically as W00143 and tags the grammar role here.

G041_masenka_invitation.title: 〜ませんか — invitation
G041_masenka_invitation.long: Adding か to the polite negative 〜ません creates a soft, polite invitation: 'won't you...?' / 'shall we...?'. Distinct from a yes/no question; the verb is negated formally but the speaker is inviting the listener.

G042_itsu_when.title: いつ — when
G042_itsu_when.long: いつ (itsu) is the interrogative noun for 'when'. Typically pairs with か at the end of the sentence: いつ来ますか (when will you come?). Like 何 and 誰 and どこ, it doesn't take a particle when used as a temporal adjunct.

G043_kosoado_pre_nominal.title: あの・この (こ/そ/あ pre-nominal) — that / this
G043_kosoado_pre_nominal.short: pre-nominal demonstratives modifying a following noun
G043_kosoado_pre_nominal.long: あの (distal: that over there), この (proximal: this here), and その (medial: that near you) precede a noun directly: あの花 (that flower), この本 (this book). Distinct from これ/それ/あれ which stand alone. Story 51 introduces あの and この; その joins later. All share catalog entry N5_kosoado.

G044_te_kudasai.title: 〜てください — please do
G044_te_kudasai.long: Adding ください after the te-form (G007_te_form) creates a polite request: please do X. 入ってください (please come in), 座ってください (please sit). Builds directly on te-form which is already familiar from stories 19+. The kanji 下さい is sometimes written, but kana ください is more common in modern usage.

G045_nan_what.title: 何 — what (interrogative)
G045_nan_what.long: 何 (nan/nani) is the interrogative noun for 'what'. Reads as nan- before -d-/-t-/-n- (何ですか nandesuka) and as nani elsewhere (何が nani-ga). The vocabulary word W00135 was introduced in story 45 but never tagged as a grammar point — story 53 promotes it and back-tags stories 45 and 51. Pairs with か as 何ですか (what is it?) and with の as 何の本 (what kind of book?).

G046_masen_deshita.title: 〜ませんでした — polite past negative
G046_masen_deshita.long: Adding でした after the polite negative 〜ません creates the past negative: 'did not (do)'. The 'didn't wait' opposite of 待ちました. Builds directly on G013_mashita_past and G036_masen — both familiar. ありませんでした also works for non-existence in the past.

G047_i_adj_past.title: 〜かった — i-adj polite past
G047_i_adj_past.long: i-adjectives form past tense by replacing the final い with かった: 暑い → 暑かった (was hot), 古い → 古かった (was old), 嬉しい → 嬉しかった (was happy). Often followed by です for politeness: 暑かったです. The kkata pattern is the canonical N5 i-adj past form.

G049_ga_but.title: 〜が — but (clause connector)
G049_ga_but.short: clause-final が meaning 'but' (less abrupt than でも)
G049_ga_but.long: Clause-final が connects two clauses with a contrastive 'but' meaning. Softer and more formal than でも (which begins a sentence). Pattern: [clause A]が、[clause B]. Example: 雨が降りますが、明るいです (it rains, but it is bright). Distinct from G002_ga_subject (subject marker) — context disambiguates because clause-が follows a verb/adjective+です/copula.

G050_he_direction.title: 〜へ — direction marker
G050_he_direction.short: particle へ marks direction/destination toward a place
G050_he_direction.long: Particle へ (pronounced 'e') marks the direction or destination of motion. Pattern: [place]へ[motion verb]. Often interchangeable with に for destinations, but へ emphasizes the direction (the trajectory, not the goal). Pronounced 'e' not 'he'. Example: 本の店へ行きます (I go toward the bookstore). Distinct from に (G004_ni_location which marks static location, time, or goal).

G059_dakara.title: だから — so / therefore
G059_dakara.long: だから appears at the start of a sentence to mean 'so / therefore', linking back to the previous statement as cause. Stronger and more conversational than embedding 〜から in a single sentence: instead of 'Aから、B' (one sentence), だから splits into 'A. だからB.' (two sentences). The narrator can stand back from the cause and assert the conclusion. Polite register usually keeps です/ます endings after だから. Example: 雪が静かです。だから私は雪が好きです。 — Snow is quiet. So I love snow.

G052_made_until.title: まで — until / as far as
G052_made_until.short: Marks the endpoint of a span in space or time.
G052_made_until.long: The particle まで marks the endpoint of an extent — either spatial ('as far as the station') or temporal ('until evening'). Pattern: [noun]まで. Frequently paired with から to express a full span: 朝から夜まで ('from morning until night'), 家から駅まで ('from home to the station'). After a place noun, まで marks the destination/limit of motion (often with 歩く, 行く, 帰る). After a time noun, it marks the cutoff of an action's duration. Distinct from に (arrival point, single moment) and から (origin/starting point — the natural counterpart of まで).
examples: 家から駅まで歩きます — I walk from my house to the station.
夕方まで友達と話しました — I talked with my friend until evening.
公園まで歩きました — I walked as far as the park.
G052_made_until.notes: Authoring tip: まで is the natural counterpart of から (origin → endpoint). Use after place nouns with motion verbs (歩く, 行く, 帰る) for spatial endpoints, and after time nouns (晩, 夕方, 朝) for temporal cutoffs. Don't confuse with までに (deadline 'by') — that's a separate later point.
